title: Classifying data as categorical or numerical
problem: Shirt sizes worn by students in a class are recorded as S, M, L, or XL. Classify this data.

steps.instruction: Ask: does the data describe qualities or labels, or does it consist of measured or counted numbers?
steps.working: Shirt sizes S, M, L, XL are labels — they describe a quality, not a number.
Because the data uses labels rather than numbers, it is categorical data.

title: Classifying numerical data as discrete or continuous
problem: The mass of each apple in a box is measured in grams. Classify this numerical data as discrete or continuous.

steps.working: Discrete data can only take specific values (usually whole numbers). Continuous data can take any value within a range.

steps.instruction: Decide whether mass can take any value or only specific values.
steps.working: Mass can be measured to any level of precision: 142 g, 142.5 g, 142.53 g, and so on.
Since mass can take any value within a range, it is continuous data.

title: Choosing an appropriate display
problem: A teacher records the number of students absent from school each day over 20 school days. Values range from 0 to 8. Choose the most appropriate display.

steps.working: Number of students absent is a count. Counts can only be whole numbers (0, 1, 2, ...), so this is discrete numerical data.

steps.instruction: Consider suitable displays for discrete numerical data with a small range.
steps.working: A dot plot places a dot above a number line for each data value. It shows individual values and the distribution clearly when the range is small and the data set is not too large.
With 20 data values and a range of 0 to 8, a dot plot is most appropriate. It shows where values are concentrated and any gaps or outliers.
